data.eyebrow: Awarded Best App for Customer Experience
data.title: Profita
data.tagline: One app. Three investors. No compromises.
data.subtitle: An investment app that serves three completely different types of investor — novice, intermediate, and values-driven — without building three separate products.

data.label: Case Study
data.title: Profita — One system. Three investment identities.
data.overview: Profita is an investment app from Land and Houses Bank. It lets users choose mutual funds, access ROBO advisory, and manage their portfolio in one place — with Morningstar ratings, historical performance, and professional fund recommendations built in. The design challenge wasn't the feature list. It was building a single experience that felt personally built for three completely different types of investor.

award.title: Best App for Customer Experience
award.event: Retail Banker International Asia Trailblazer Awards 2023

data.headline: Building for the least confident investor was the strategy, not the compromise.
data.body: The conventional approach to an investment app for 'all experience levels' is to build for the median user — someone who knows a little, wants a little guidance, and won't be intimidated by a performance chart. Profita rejected that. The bet was to build genuinely for the novice investor: make the app so welcoming and clear that someone with zero investment knowledge could make their first trade with confidence. The hypothesis was that if you build for the most underserved user without condescending to the experienced one, the resulting design earns trust across the entire spectrum. Three years later, the award validated it.

data.title: Design brief
data.body: Design a mutual fund app that offers unique funding experiences based on user profiles and preferences, regardless of their previous investment experience. Utilize AI technology to personalize the user experience.

questions.text: How can the app create a
questions.suffix: for users who are new to investing?

questions.highlight: intermediate and experienced investors optimize and diversify
ensure a seamless and intuitive experience
personalize the investment experience and keep users engaged

data.title: Getting to know the user & understanding the context

methods.label: User interviews
methods.body: I consider myself a novice investor, which led me to start with empathy rather than assumptions. I asked open-ended questions about daily life, financial goals, and attitudes toward money — not just app preferences. I interviewed four individuals across three user groups: Novice Investors, Intermediate Investors, and values-driven investors. The goal was to find where confidence and confusion diverge across experience levels.

data.text: How might we create a welcoming and educational onboarding experience for new users in the mutual fund app?

items.headline: Control creates anxiety
items.body: Users wanted to feel in control of their investments — but exercising that control was exactly what scared them. Being shown a fund selection interface as a novice didn't feel empowering. It felt like a test they hadn't studied for. The design had to give the illusion of control while reducing the actual burden of decision-making.

items.headline: What's educational for one is condescending to another
items.body: The same onboarding flow that calmed Sarocha frustrated Anan. A glossary tooltip on 'Sharpe ratio' was helpful for a novice and patronizing for a financial analyst. Information density and guidance level couldn't be static — the app had to adapt without asking users to configure it manually.

items.headline: Values vs. returns is a false tradeoff — but only if you design for it
items.body: ESG-focused investors didn't want to sacrifice returns for values. They wanted to see evidence that sustainable funds could compete. If ESG was just a filter that removed everything else from view, it looked like a values-driven sacrifice. The design had to frame it as intelligent selection, not restriction.

data.title: Three investors. Three completely different first taps.

items.name: Sarocha T.
items.biography: Recently started her career in marketing. Wants to invest but finds financial language intimidating. Has a savings goal — a home purchase — but doesn't know how to translate that into a fund selection.
items.goal: Make her first investment without feeling like she's guessing. Build a simple portfolio that moves toward a home purchase over 3–5 years.
items.needs: An automated path that makes the first decision for her. Clear language with no assumed knowledge. A goal-framing entry point ('save for a home') rather than a fund-browsing entry point.
items.frustrations: Opens a fund comparison screen and sees 'Sharpe ratio: 0.81', '3Y return: 12.4%', 'standard deviation: 6.2'. Has no idea if any of those numbers are good. Closes the app.
items.why_use: Sarocha opens the app and taps 'Let ROBO Advisor handle it.' She sets a goal: save ฿500,000 in 3 years. ROBO recommends a conservative mixed-fund portfolio. She confirms without reading the fund details — the ROBO path is designed so she doesn't have to. She invests in under 4 minutes. She comes back monthly to check progress against her goal, not to rebalance her portfolio.

items.name: Anan
items.biography: Financial analyst with 4 years of investing experience. Has an existing portfolio on another platform and wants to diversify into categories it doesn't cover well. Comes to Profita for specific access, not for guidance.
items.goal: Find funds in underrepresented categories — Asia ex-Japan equity, global bonds — and add them to a portfolio he actively manages.
items.needs: Raw performance data accessible from the fund card, not behind a detail page. Morningstar rating visible as one data point among several, not the dominant signal. Quick comparison between 2–3 shortlisted funds.
items.frustrations: Platforms that treat him like a beginner. A 5-step onboarding flow that teaches him what a mutual fund is. Tooltips explaining basic terms he's known for years.
items.why_use: Anan skips the ROBO path at onboarding. He goes directly to Fund Discovery, filters by 4-star Morningstar and Asia ex-Japan region, and gets 8 results. He opens the top two funds, checks their 3-year return chart and expense ratio side-by-side, and invests in the better performer. The whole session takes 6 minutes. He doesn't need the educational content — he needs the app to stay out of his way.

items.name: Oraya W.
items.biography: Environmental scientist. Treats investment as an extension of her values — won't invest in fossil fuels, tobacco, or companies with poor governance scores. Has been frustrated by platforms that treat ESG as a niche toggle rather than a primary lens.
items.goal: Build a portfolio that reflects her values without sacrificing competitive returns. Prove to herself that sustainable investing doesn't require accepting lower performance.
items.needs: ESG filter that restructures the entire fund catalogue — not a tag on cards she still has to sort through. Impact scorecard showing CO2, governance, and social metrics per fund. Evidence that the ESG funds she's considering have performance comparable to non-ESG alternatives.
items.frustrations: Platforms that show ESG as a tag among 15 other tags. Having to manually exclude fossil fuel funds one by one. No way to see how an ESG fund's returns compare to the broader category average.
items.why_use: Oraya opens the app and taps the ESG filter first. The catalogue collapses from 200+ funds to 14. She sorts by impact scorecard — a composite of CO2 reduction, governance score, and social impact. She opens the top-ranked fund, checks its 5-year performance against the category benchmark (6.1% vs 5.8% average), and invests. The filter didn't hide performance data — it just ensured she only ever had to look at funds she'd actually consider.

data.title: Big ideas & feature prioritization
data.description: Together with our users, we identified features that directly address their needs and capitalize on opportunities from our research.

features.name: Educational Onboarding Wizard
features.rationale: Essential for guiding Sarocha through her initial investment journey

features.name: ROBO Advisor Path
features.rationale: Removes the fund-selection decision entirely for users who can't yet make it confidently

features.name: Goal-Based Portfolio View
features.rationale: Frames progress in terms Sarocha understands — 'you're 34% toward your home purchase goal'

features.name: Investment Simulations
features.rationale: Useful for building confidence — best introduced after first investment is made

features.name: Advanced Analytics Toolkit
features.rationale: Crucial for Anan's evaluation process — raw performance data must be surface-level, not hidden

features.name: Morningstar Rating Integration
features.rationale: Third-party credibility signal useful across all levels — front-and-center for novices, one input among several for Anan

features.name: Fund Comparison Tool
features.rationale: Anan's critical workflow — side-by-side comparison of 2–3 shortlisted funds

features.name: Real-Time Market Alerts
features.rationale: Useful for active management, but secondary to the comparison and discovery tools

features.name: ESG View-Restructuring Filter
features.rationale: ESG as a primary filter that collapses the catalogue — not a tag on individual cards

features.name: Impact Scorecard per Fund
features.rationale: CO2, governance, and social impact as visible metrics alongside financial performance

features.name: ESG vs. Category Benchmark Comparison
features.rationale: Addresses Oraya's core tension — she needs evidence that sustainable doesn't mean lower returns

features.name: Sustainability Reports
features.rationale: Supports ongoing transparency but secondary to the initial fund selection decision

data.headline: Four choices that made one app feel like three different products.

items.title: ROBO Advisor as opt-in path, not the default
items.problem: Novice investors don't know how to select funds. But making ROBO the default removes the sense of agency from intermediate investors who came specifically to manage their own portfolio.

options.text: ROBO as default, manual opt-out — lowest friction for novices
Manual browsing as default, ROBO as an explicit mode to switch into
Adaptive — experience-level question at onboarding routes each user to a different starting point

items.rationale: Routes users appropriately from the first tap without forcing either group to fight the default.
items.cost: Experience level is self-reported. A novice who answers 'intermediate' to seem confident skips ROBO and lands in the interface designed for Anan, not Sarocha.

items.title: Morningstar star as the leading credibility signal — not raw returns
items.problem: Novice users saw '3-year return: 12.4%' and 'Sharpe ratio: 0.81' and asked: 'is that good?' They had no reference frame. Every comparison was guesswork.

options.text: Show all performance data upfront — clean, doesn't patronize experienced users
Front-load the Morningstar star rating (1–5) as a recognised third-party signal; detailed data accessible below

items.rationale: Gives novice users a reference point without requiring any financial literacy.
items.cost: Experienced investors who distrust Morningstar find the star system slightly in their way. The interface asks Anan to look past it.

items.title: ESG as a view-restructuring filter, not a tag
items.problem: ESG-focused investors don't use sustainability as a preference toggle — it's a qualifying criterion. If ESG is a tag on cards, users still scroll through 200+ funds to find the 14 that qualify.

options.text: ESG tag on individual fund cards — visible, but user still browses the full catalogue
ESG as a primary filter that collapses the entire catalogue to qualifying funds only, immediately

items.rationale: Restructures discovery for users who would never invest outside ESG regardless.
items.cost: Users who want to compare ESG vs non-ESG performance can't do that while the filter is active. It's all-or-nothing.

items.title: Implicit profiling through behaviour, not onboarding surveys
items.problem: AI personalisation requires knowing what a user likes. An onboarding survey asking 'what funds interest you?' produces aspirational answers, not accurate ones.

options.text: Detailed onboarding survey — builds profile upfront, enables immediate personalisation
Minimal profile at signup; learn from every interaction — what users save, skip, compare

items.rationale: Behavioral data is more accurate than stated preferences. The system learns without asking.
items.cost: The first several sessions feel generic. Users who churn after 3 sessions never experience the intelligence. Personalisation is a long-game payoff.

data.headline: AI cuts portfolio risk by 62% — automatically.
data.subtitle: Same ฿500,000 goal. One portfolio built by gut instinct, one composed by the AI. The difference is visible.

data.headline: One app. Three completely different experiences.
data.description: The design challenge wasn't building features for each user — it was building one system that adapts. Switch between personas to feel the difference.

data.title: Navigating Opportunities
data.description: The fund discovery flow guides users from initial search through detailed evaluation to completed investment — adapting based on user preferences and risk profile.
steps: Novice → ROBO path / Intermediate → Fund Discovery
Set goal or browse by category

data.headline: Best App for Customer Experience — Retail Banker International Asia, 2023.
data.body:
Three years after launch, Profita was recognised at the Retail Banker International Asia Trailblazer Awards — not for its technical capabilities, but for how it made customers feel.

The recognition validated the core design bet: that building for the least confident user would raise the bar for everyone. The onboarding experience Sarocha went through was the same one Anan and Oraya went through. Making it genuinely welcoming — not just functional — crossed experience levels and earned the recognition.

The adaptive design didn't treat users as permanent categories. It treated the novice investor as someone who might become an intermediate investor one day — and designed for that journey, not just the first session.

data.text: The novice investor is the hardest design brief: she needs confidence she doesn't have, in a language she hasn't learned. Build for her without condescending to the expert, and you've solved something most fintech doesn't bother attempting.
